# Diffusion Models

## Core Intuition

- Start from structured data.
- Gradually add noise until the data is nearly random.
- Learn to reverse this process by denoising.

TODO: Insert diffusion noising/denoising diagram at `figures/diffusion_process.png`.

---

## From Images to Actions

Diffusion models are widely known for image generation, but the same idea can generate:

- robot joint commands
- end-effector poses
- action trajectories
- control parameters

The sample space changes; the denoising principle remains.

---

## Forward Noising Process

A common forward process is:

$$
q(x_k \mid x_{k-1})
=
\mathcal{N}
\left(
x_k;
\sqrt{1-\beta_k}x_{k-1},
\beta_k I
\right)
$$

where $k$ indexes diffusion time, not robot time.

---

## Reverse Denoising Process

The learned model predicts how to move from noisy samples toward clean data:

$$
p_\theta(x_{k-1} \mid x_k, c)
$$

where $c$ is optional conditioning information such as images, proprioception, or task context.

---

## Noise Prediction Objective

A standard training objective predicts the injected noise:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{diff}(\theta)
=
\mathbb{E}_{x_0,\epsilon,k}
\left[
\lVert
\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_k, k, c)
\rVert^2
\right]
$$

---

## Sampling Bottleneck

- Sampling requires repeated denoising steps.
- More steps usually improve sample quality.
- Robot control also requires bounded latency.

This tradeoff becomes central when diffusion models are used as policies.

---

## DDPM vs DDIM

- DDPM: stochastic denoising sampler.
- DDIM: deterministic or partially deterministic sampler with fewer steps.
- TODO: Add comparison table at `figures/ddpm_ddim_comparison.png`.
